file.content:
# Makefile for sinf accuracy test — A/B comparison
# Target: RV32IM soft-float (Andes optimized assembly)
#
# Usage:
#   make              — build both opt and ref ELFs
#   make compare      — build, run both in QEMU, diff outputs

TOOLCHAIN = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
PREFIX    = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-

CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc
AS  = $(PREFIX)gcc
LD  = $(PREFIX)gcc

ARCH    = -march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32
CFLAGS  = $(ARCH) -O2 -mvh -Wall
ASFLAGS = $(ARCH)
LDFLAGS = $(ARCH) -mvh -lm

QEMU = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios

# Assembly source under test (dependencies come from toolchain libc)
ASM_SRCS = sf_sin.s
ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)

TEST_SRC = test_sinf.c
TEST_OBJ = test_sinf.o

OPT_ELF = test_sinf_opt.elf
REF_ELF = test_sinf_ref.elf

all: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)

# Optimized: link with Andes assembly (objects before -lm to override)
$(OPT_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS) $(LDFLAGS)

# Reference: link with toolchain's libm only (no assembly)
$(REF_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(LDFLAGS)

$(TEST_OBJ): $(TEST_SRC)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

%.o: %.s
	$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

SIZE = $(PREFIX)size
NM   = $(PREFIX)nm

REF_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.ref.o)

%.ref.o: %.s
	git show HEAD:libm/machine/riscv/$< > $*.ref.s
	$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $*.ref.s
	rm -f $*.ref.s

size: $(ASM_OBJS) $(REF_OBJS)
	@echo "=== sinf .text size ==="
	@printf "  opt: %d bytes\n" $$($(SIZE) -A sf_sin.o | awk '/\.text/ {print $$2}')
	@printf "  ref: %d bytes\n" $$($(SIZE) -A sf_sin.ref.o | awk '/\.text/ {print $$2}')

compare: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)
	@echo "=== Running reference (toolchain libm) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(REF_ELF) > ref_output.txt
	@echo "=== Running optimized (Andes assembly) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(OPT_ELF) > opt_output.txt
	@echo "=== Diff (< = ref, > = opt) ==="
	@diff ref_output.txt opt_output.txt > diff_output.txt && echo "IDENTICAL" || \
		(echo "$$(wc -l < diff_output.txt) diff lines — see diff_output.txt"; head -40 diff_output.txt)

clean:
	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) $(REF_OBJS) $(TEST_OBJ) $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF) \
	      ref_output.txt opt_output.txt diff_output.txt

.PHONY: all size compare clean

content.text:
The `make size` target (lines 59–62) works like this:

1. **Builds two `.o` files** from `sf_sin.s`:
   - `sf_sin.o` — assembled from the current working copy (your optimized version)
   - `sf_sin.ref.o` — assembled from the **last committed** version (`git show HEAD:...`)

2. **Runs `riscv32-elf-size -A`** on each object file with the `-A` (SysV format) flag, which prints section sizes in bytes.

3. **Extracts the `.text` section size** using `awk '/\.text/ {print $2}'` and prints both as `opt` vs `ref` byte counts.

So it's comparing the `.text` (code) section size of your working-tree assembly against the HEAD commit's version — a quick before/after delta for code size impact.

# Makefile for sinf accuracy test — A/B comparison
# Target: RV32IM soft-float (Andes optimized assembly)
#
# Usage:
#   make              — build both opt and ref ELFs
#   make compare      — build, run both in QEMU, diff outputs

TOOLCHAIN = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
PREFIX    = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-

CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc

ARCH    = -march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32
CFLAGS  = $(ARCH) -O2 -mvh -Wall

QEMU = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios

# Assembly sources for size reporting
ASM_SRCS = sf_atan.s sf_ceil.s sf_cos.s sf_fabs.s sf_floor.s sf_frexp.s \
           sf_ldexp.s sf_modf.s sf_sin.s sf_tan.s sf_tanh.s \
           sf_acos.s sf_asin.s sf_cosh.s sf_exp.s sf_fmod.s sf_log.s \
           sf_log10.s sf_pow.s sf_sinh.s sf_sqrt.s \
           sf_fpclassify.s
ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)

# C sources for size reporting
C_SRCS = sf_sincos.c sf_scalbn.c
C_OBJS = $(C_SRCS:.c=.o)

TEST_SRC = test_sinf.c
TEST_OBJ = test_sinf.o

OPT_ELF = test_sinf_opt.elf
REF_ELF = test_sinf_ref.elf

all: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)

# Optimized: link with Andes assembly (objects before -lm to override)
$(OPT_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS) $(LDFLAGS)

# Reference: link with toolchain's libm only (no assembly)
$(REF_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(LDFLAGS)

$(TEST_OBJ): $(TEST_SRC)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

%.o: %.s
	$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

SIZE = $(PREFIX)size

	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

	@echo "=== .text size (bytes) ==="

		$$($(SIZE) -A $(ASM_OBJS) $(C_OBJS) | awk '/\.text/ {s+=$$2} END {print s}')

compare: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)
	@echo "=== Running reference (toolchain libm) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(REF_ELF) > ref_output.txt
	@echo "=== Running optimized (Andes assembly) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(OPT_ELF) > opt_output.txt
	@echo "=== Diff (< = ref, > = opt) ==="

clean:
	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) $(TEST_OBJ) $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF) \
	      ref_output.txt opt_output.txt diff_output.txt

.PHONY: all size compare clean

lines: (echo "$$(wc -l < diff_output.txt) diff lines — see diff_output.txt"; head -40 diff_output.txt)

content.text:
The explicit `$(TEST_OBJ)` rule on line 51 will still take priority for `test_sinf.o`, so no conflict. Now the `%.o: %.c` pattern rule handles the C size sources. But the C files need `-O2` without `-mvh` since they're not test harnesses — actually `CFLAGS` already has `-O2` which is fine for size measurement, and `-mvh` won't affect code generation, just linking. This is fine.

content.text: These C files depend on picolibc internal headers (`fdlibm.h`) that aren't available in the standalone toolchain build. The simplest approach is to drop the C sources from the `size` target since they need the full picolibc build environment. Let me adjust:

input.old_string:
# C sources for size reporting
C_SRCS = sf_sincos.c sf_scalbn.c
C_OBJS = $(C_SRCS:.c=.o)

toolUseResult.oldString:
# C sources for size reporting
C_SRCS = sf_sincos.c sf_scalbn.c
C_OBJS = $(C_SRCS:.c=.o)
toolUseResult.newString:
# C sources (need picolibc headers, excluded from standalone size target)
# sf_sincos.c, sf_scalbn.c, sf_fpclassify — measured via full meson build
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Makefile for sinf accuracy test — A/B comparison
# Target: RV32IM soft-float (Andes optimized assembly)
#
# Usage:
#   make              — build both opt and ref ELFs
#   make compare      — build, run both in QEMU, diff outputs

TOOLCHAIN = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
PREFIX    = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-

CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc
AS  = $(PREFIX)gcc
LD  = $(PREFIX)gcc

ARCH    = -march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32
CFLAGS  = $(ARCH) -O2 -mvh -Wall
ASFLAGS = $(ARCH)
LDFLAGS = $(ARCH) -mvh -lm

QEMU = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios

# Assembly sources for size reporting
ASM_SRCS = sf_atan.s sf_ceil.s sf_cos.s sf_fabs.s sf_floor.s sf_frexp.s \
           sf_ldexp.s sf_modf.s sf_sin.s sf_tan.s sf_tanh.s \
           sf_acos.s sf_asin.s sf_cosh.s sf_exp.s sf_fmod.s sf_log.s \
           sf_log10.s sf_pow.s sf_sinh.s sf_sqrt.s \
           sf_fpclassify.s
ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)

# C sources for size reporting
C_SRCS = sf_sincos.c sf_scalbn.c
C_OBJS = $(C_SRCS:.c=.o)

TEST_SRC = test_sinf.c
TEST_OBJ = test_sinf.o

OPT_ELF = test_sinf_opt.elf
REF_ELF = test_sinf_ref.elf

all: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)

# Optimized: link with Andes assembly (objects before -lm to override)
$(OPT_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS) $(LDFLAGS)

# Reference: link with toolchain's libm only (no assembly)
$(REF_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(LDFLAGS)

$(TEST_OBJ): $(TEST_SRC)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

%.o: %.s
	$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

SIZE = $(PREFIX)size
NM   = $(PREFIX)nm

%.o: %.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

size: $(ASM_OBJS) $(C_OBJS)
	@echo "=== .text size (bytes) ==="
	@$(foreach o,$(ASM_OBJS) $(C_OBJS), \
		printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(basename $(o)) \
			$$($(SIZE) -A $(o) | awk '/\.text/ {print $$2}');)
	@echo "  --------------------"
	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" "TOTAL" \
		$$($(SIZE) -A $(ASM_OBJS) $(C_OBJS) | awk '/\.text/ {s+=$$2} END {print s}')

compare: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)
	@echo "=== Running reference (toolchain libm) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(REF_ELF) > ref_output.txt
	@echo "=== Running optimized (Andes assembly) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(OPT_ELF) > opt_output.txt
	@echo "=== Diff (< = ref, > = opt) ==="
	@diff ref_output.txt opt_output.txt > diff_output.txt && echo "IDENTICAL" || \
		(echo "$$(wc -l < diff_output.txt) diff lines — see diff_output.txt"; head -40 diff_output.txt)

clean:
	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) $(C_OBJS) $(TEST_OBJ) $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF) \
	      ref_output.txt opt_output.txt diff_output.txt

.PHONY: all size compare clean

# Assembly sources for size reporting
ASM_SRCS = sf_atan.s sf_ceil.s sf_cos.s sf_fabs.s sf_floor.s sf_frexp.s \
           sf_ldexp.s sf_modf.s sf_sin.s sf_tan.s sf_tanh.s \
           sf_acos.s sf_asin.s sf_cosh.s sf_exp.s sf_fmod.s sf_log.s \
           sf_log10.s sf_pow.s sf_sinh.s sf_sqrt.s \
           sf_fpclassify.s
ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)

# Note: sf_sincos.c and sf_scalbn.c need picolibc headers, excluded from
# standalone size target. Measure those via full meson build.

TEST_SRC = test_sinf.c
TEST_OBJ = test_sinf.o

OPT_ELF = test_sinf_opt.elf
REF_ELF = test_sinf_ref.elf

all: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)

# Optimized: link with Andes assembly (objects before -lm to override)
$(OPT_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS) $(LDFLAGS)

# Reference: link with toolchain's libm only (no assembly)
$(REF_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(LDFLAGS)

$(TEST_OBJ): $(TEST_SRC)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

%.o: %.s
	$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

SIZE = $(PREFIX)size
NM   = $(PREFIX)nm

size: $(ASM_OBJS)
	@echo "=== .text size (bytes) ==="
	@$(foreach o,$(ASM_OBJS), \
		printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(basename $(o)) \
			$$($(SIZE) -A $(o) | awk '/\.text/ {print $$2}');)
	@echo "  --------------------"
	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" "TOTAL" \
		$$($(SIZE) -A $(ASM_OBJS) | awk '/\.text/ {s+=$$2} END {print s}')

compare: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)
	@echo "=== Running reference (toolchain libm) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(REF_ELF) > ref_output.txt
	@echo "=== Running optimized (Andes assembly) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(OPT_ELF) > opt_output.txt
	@echo "=== Diff (< = ref, > = opt) ==="
	@diff ref_output.txt opt_output.txt > diff_output.txt && echo "IDENTICAL" || \
		(echo "$$(wc -l < diff_output.txt) diff lines — see diff_output.txt"; head -40 diff_output.txt)

clean:
	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) $(C_OBJS) $(TEST_OBJ) $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF) \
	      ref_output.txt opt_output.txt diff_output.txt

.PHONY: all size compare clean

lines: (echo "$$(wc -l < diff_output.txt) diff lines — see diff_output.txt"; head -40 diff_output.txt)

file.content:
# Makefile for sinf accuracy test — A/B comparison
# Target: RV32IM soft-float (Andes optimized assembly)
#
# Usage:
#   make              — build both opt and ref ELFs
#   make compare      — build, run both in QEMU, diff outputs

TOOLCHAIN = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
PREFIX    = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-

CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc
AS  = $(PREFIX)gcc
LD  = $(PREFIX)gcc

ARCH    = -march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32
CFLAGS  = $(ARCH) -O2 -mvh -Wall
ASFLAGS = $(ARCH)
LDFLAGS = $(ARCH) -mvh -lm

QEMU = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios

# Assembly sources for size reporting
ASM_SRCS = sf_atan.s sf_ceil.s sf_cos.s sf_fabs.s sf_floor.s sf_frexp.s \
           sf_ldexp.s sf_modf.s sf_sin.s sf_tan.s sf_tanh.s \
           sf_acos.s sf_asin.s sf_cosh.s sf_exp.s sf_fmod.s sf_log.s \
           sf_log10.s sf_pow.s sf_sinh.s sf_sqrt.s \
           sf_fpclassify.s
ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)

# Note: sf_sincos.c and sf_scalbn.c need picolibc headers, excluded from
# standalone size target. Measure those via full meson build.

TEST_SRC = test_sinf.c
TEST_OBJ = test_sinf.o

OPT_ELF = test_sinf_opt.elf
REF_ELF = test_sinf_ref.elf

all: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)

# Optimized: link with Andes assembly (objects before -lm to override)
$(OPT_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS) $(LDFLAGS)

# Reference: link with toolchain's libm only (no assembly)
$(REF_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(LDFLAGS)

$(TEST_OBJ): $(TEST_SRC)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

%.o: %.s
	$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

SIZE = $(PREFIX)size
NM   = $(PREFIX)nm

size: $(ASM_OBJS)
	@echo "=== .text size (bytes) ==="
	@$(foreach o,$(ASM_OBJS), \
		printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(basename $(o)) \
			$$($(SIZE) -A $(o) | awk '/\.text/ {print $$2}');)
	@echo "  --------------------"
	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" "TOTAL" \
		$$($(SIZE) -A $(ASM_OBJS) | awk '/\.text/ {s+=$$2} END {print s}')

compare: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)
	@echo "=== Running reference (toolchain libm) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(REF_ELF) > ref_output.txt
	@echo "=== Running optimized (Andes assembly) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(OPT_ELF) > opt_output.txt
	@echo "=== Diff (< = ref, > = opt) ==="
	@diff ref_output.txt opt_output.txt > diff_output.txt && echo "IDENTICAL" || \
		(echo "$$(wc -l < diff_output.txt) diff lines — see diff_output.txt"; head -40 diff_output.txt)

clean:
	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) $(TEST_OBJ) $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF) \
	      ref_output.txt opt_output.txt diff_output.txt

.PHONY: all size compare clean

input.content:
# Makefile for libm accuracy test — A/B comparison
# Target: RV32IM soft-float (Andes optimized assembly)
#
# Usage:
#   make size           — report .text size for each assembly function
#   make compare        — build, run opt vs ref in QEMU, diff outputs
#   make compare-sinf   — compare a single function

TOOLCHAIN = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
PREFIX    = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-

CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc
AS  = $(PREFIX)gcc
LD  = $(PREFIX)gcc

ARCH    = -march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32
CFLAGS  = $(ARCH) -O2 -mvh -Wall
ASFLAGS = $(ARCH)
LDFLAGS = $(ARCH) -mvh -lm

QEMU = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios

SIZE = $(PREFIX)size
NM   = $(PREFIX)nm

# Assembly sources
ASM_SRCS = sf_atan.s sf_ceil.s sf_cos.s sf_fabs.s sf_floor.s sf_frexp.s \
           sf_ldexp.s sf_modf.s sf_sin.s sf_tan.s sf_tanh.s \
           sf_acos.s sf_asin.s sf_cosh.s sf_exp.s sf_fmod.s sf_log.s \
           sf_log10.s sf_pow.s sf_sinh.s sf_sqrt.s \
           sf_fpclassify.s
ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)

# Note: sf_sincos.c and sf_scalbn.c need picolibc headers, excluded from
# standalone size target. Measure those via full meson build.

# Functions to test (must have test_<name>.c and sf_<name>.s)
FUNCS = sinf cosf tanf atanf fabsf ceilf floorf expf sinhf coshf tanhf \
        logf log10f sqrtf asinf acosf fmodf powf frexpf ldexpf modff \
        fpclassifyf

# --- Pattern rules ---

%.o: %.s
	$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

%.o: %.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

# For each function, build opt (with asm) and ref (toolchain only) ELFs.
# The asm object name: strip trailing 'f' from func name to get sf_<base>.o
# e.g. sinf -> sf_sin.o, log10f -> sf_log10.o, fpclassifyf -> sf_fpclassify.o
define FUNC_RULES
test_$(1)_opt.elf: test_$(1).o sf_$(patsubst %f,%,$(1)).o
	$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)

test_$(1)_ref.elf: test_$(1).o
	$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)

compare-$(1): test_$(1)_opt.elf test_$(1)_ref.elf
	@$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) test_$(1)_ref.elf > test_$(1)_ref.txt
	@$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) test_$(1)_opt.elf > test_$(1)_opt.txt
	@if diff -q test_$(1)_ref.txt test_$(1)_opt.txt > /dev/null 2>&1; then \
		printf "  %-14s PASS\n" $(1); \
	else \
		printf "  %-14s FAIL (%d diffs)\n" $(1) \
			$$$$(diff test_$(1)_ref.txt test_$(1)_opt.txt | grep -c '^[<>]'); \
	fi
endef

$(foreach f,$(FUNCS),$(eval $(call FUNC_RULES,$(f))))

# --- Top-level targets ---

OPT_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test_$(f)_opt.elf)
REF_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test_$(f)_ref.elf)

all: $(OPT_ELFS) $(REF_ELFS)

compare: $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),compare-$(f))
	@echo "Done."

size: $(ASM_OBJS)
	@echo "=== .text size (bytes) ==="
	@$(foreach o,$(ASM_OBJS), \
		printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(basename $(o)) \
			$$($(SIZE) -A $(o) | awk '/\.text/ {print $$2}');)
	@echo "  --------------------"
	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" "TOTAL" \
		$$($(SIZE) -A $(ASM_OBJS) | awk '/\.text/ {s+=$$2} END {print s}')

clean:
	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) *.o *.elf *_ref.txt *_opt.txt

.PHONY: all size compare clean $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),compare-$(f))

#   make compare      — build, run both in QEMU, diff outputs

TOOLCHAIN = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
PREFIX    = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-

CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc
AS  = $(PREFIX)gcc
LD  = $(PREFIX)gcc

ARCH    = -march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32
CFLAGS  = $(ARCH) -O2 -mvh -Wall
ASFLAGS = $(ARCH)
LDFLAGS = $(ARCH) -mvh -lm

QEMU = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios

# Assembly sources for size reporting
ASM_SRCS = sf_atan.s sf_ceil.s sf_cos.s sf_fabs.s sf_floor.s sf_frexp.s \
           sf_ldexp.s sf_modf.s sf_sin.s sf_tan.s sf_tanh.s \
           sf_acos.s sf_asin.s sf_cosh.s sf_exp.s sf_fmod.s sf_log.s \
           sf_log10.s sf_pow.s sf_sinh.s sf_sqrt.s \
           sf_fpclassify.s
ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)

# Note: sf_sincos.c and sf_scalbn.c need picolibc headers, excluded from
# standalone size target. Measure those via full meson build.

TEST_SRC = test_sinf.c
TEST_OBJ = test_sinf.o

OPT_ELF = test_sinf_opt.elf
REF_ELF = test_sinf_ref.elf

all: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)

# Optimized: link with Andes assembly (objects before -lm to override)
$(OPT_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS) $(LDFLAGS)

# Reference: link with toolchain's libm only (no assembly)
$(REF_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(LDFLAGS)

$(TEST_OBJ): $(TEST_SRC)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

%.o: %.s
	$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

SIZE = $(PREFIX)size
NM   = $(PREFIX)nm

size: $(ASM_OBJS)
	@echo "=== .text size (bytes) ==="
	@$(foreach o,$(ASM_OBJS), \
		printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(basename $(o)) \
			$$($(SIZE) -A $(o) | awk '/\.text/ {print $$2}');)
	@echo "  --------------------"
	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" "TOTAL" \
		$$($(SIZE) -A $(ASM_OBJS) | awk '/\.text/ {s+=$$2} END {print s}')

compare: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)
	@echo "=== Running reference (toolchain libm) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(REF_ELF) > ref_output.txt
	@echo "=== Running optimized (Andes assembly) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(OPT_ELF) > opt_output.txt
	@echo "=== Diff (< = ref, > = opt) ==="
	@diff ref_output.txt opt_output.txt > diff_output.txt && echo "IDENTICAL" || \
		(echo "$$(wc -l < diff_output.txt) diff lines — see diff_output.txt"; head -40 diff_output.txt)

clean:
	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) $(TEST_OBJ) $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF) \
	      ref_output.txt opt_output.txt diff_output.txt

.PHONY: all size compare clean

file.content:
# Makefile for libm accuracy test — A/B comparison
# Target: RV32IM soft-float (Andes optimized assembly)
#
# Usage:
#   make size           — report .text size for each assembly function
#   make compare        — build, run opt vs ref in QEMU, diff outputs
#   make compare-sinf   — compare a single function

TOOLCHAIN = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
PREFIX    = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-

CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc
AS  = $(PREFIX)gcc
LD  = $(PREFIX)gcc

ARCH    = -march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32
CFLAGS  = $(ARCH) -O2 -mvh -Wall
ASFLAGS = $(ARCH)
LDFLAGS = $(ARCH) -mvh -lm

QEMU = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios

SIZE = $(PREFIX)size
NM   = $(PREFIX)nm

# Assembly sources
ASM_SRCS = sf_atan.s sf_ceil.s sf_cos.s sf_fabs.s sf_floor.s sf_frexp.s \
           sf_ldexp.s sf_modf.s sf_sin.s sf_tan.s sf_tanh.s \
           sf_acos.s sf_asin.s sf_cosh.s sf_exp.s sf_fmod.s sf_log.s \
           sf_log10.s sf_pow.s sf_sinh.s sf_sqrt.s \
           sf_fpclassify.s
ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)

# Note: sf_sincos.c and sf_scalbn.c need picolibc headers, excluded from
# standalone size target. Measure those via full meson build.

# Functions to test (must have test_<name>.c and sf_<name>.s)
FUNCS = sinf cosf tanf atanf fabsf ceilf floorf expf sinhf coshf tanhf \
        logf log10f sqrtf asinf acosf fmodf powf frexpf ldexpf modff \
        fpclassifyf

# --- Pattern rules ---

%.o: %.s
	$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

%.o: %.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

# For each function, build opt (with asm) and ref (toolchain only) ELFs.
# The asm object name: strip trailing 'f' from func name to get sf_<base>.o
# e.g. sinf -> sf_sin.o, log10f -> sf_log10.o, fpclassifyf -> sf_fpclassify.o
define FUNC_RULES
test_$(1)_opt.elf: test_$(1).o sf_$(patsubst %f,%,$(1)).o
	$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)

test_$(1)_ref.elf: test_$(1).o
	$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)

compare-$(1): test_$(1)_opt.elf test_$(1)_ref.elf
	@$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) test_$(1)_ref.elf > test_$(1)_ref.txt
	@$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) test_$(1)_opt.elf > test_$(1)_opt.txt
	@if diff -q test_$(1)_ref.txt test_$(1)_opt.txt > /dev/null 2>&1; then \
		printf "  %-14s PASS\n" $(1); \
	else \
		printf "  %-14s FAIL (%d diffs)\n" $(1) \
			$$$$(diff test_$(1)_ref.txt test_$(1)_opt.txt | grep -c '^[<>]'); \
	fi
endef

$(foreach f,$(FUNCS),$(eval $(call FUNC_RULES,$(f))))

# --- Top-level targets ---

OPT_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test_$(f)_opt.elf)
REF_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test_$(f)_ref.elf)

all: $(OPT_ELFS) $(REF_ELFS)

compare: $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),compare-$(f))
	@echo "Done."

size: $(ASM_OBJS)
	@echo "=== .text size (bytes) ==="
	@$(foreach o,$(ASM_OBJS), \
		printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(basename $(o)) \
			$$($(SIZE) -A $(o) | awk '/\.text/ {print $$2}');)
	@echo "  --------------------"
	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" "TOTAL" \
		$$($(SIZE) -A $(ASM_OBJS) | awk '/\.text/ {s+=$$2} END {print s}')

clean:
	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) *.o *.elf *_ref.txt *_opt.txt

.PHONY: all size compare clean $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),compare-$(f))

input.prompt:
I need to review all 21 RISC-V assembly files in /home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/libm/machine/riscv/ for code size optimization opportunities. The target is `-march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32` (soft-float, compressed instructions available).

Read ALL of these assembly files and analyze them for code size reduction opportunities:
sf_sin.s, sf_cos.s, sf_tan.s, sf_atan.s, sf_fabs.s, sf_ceil.s, sf_floor.s, sf_exp.s, sf_sinh.s, sf_cosh.s, sf_tanh.s, sf_log.s, sf_log10.s, sf_sqrt.s, sf_asin.s, sf_acos.s, sf_fmod.s, sf_pow.s, sf_frexp.s, sf_ldexp.s, sf_modf.s, sf_fpclassify.s

For each file, look for:
1. Instructions that could be replaced with RV32C compressed equivalents (16-bit instead of 32-bit). C-extension compressed instructions include: c.li, c.lui, c.mv, c.add, c.addi, c.sw, c.lw, c.lwsp, c.swsp, c.j, c.jal, c.jr, c.jalr, c.beqz, c.bnez, c.slli, c.srli, c.srai, c.andi, c.sub, c.xor, c.or, c.and, c.nop, c.addi16sp, c.addi4spn. Note: the assembler may auto-compress if the instruction can be expressed as a C instruction - check if the code uses explicit 32-bit forms that prevent compression.
2. Redundant instructions (e.g., loading a value that's never used, unnecessary moves)
3. Unnecessary stack frame overhead (pushing/popping registers that aren't modified)
4. Repeated instruction sequences that could be factored out
5. Branch sequences that could be simplified
6. Any other size reduction opportunities

IMPORTANT: These are hand-tuned assembly files originally from Andes NDS32 transliterated to RISC-V. The assembler (gas) will auto-compress eligible instructions to C-extension when possible. So focus on algorithmic/structural optimizations rather than instruction encoding.

For each file, report:
- Current .text size (from `make size` output): sf_atan=88, sf_ceil=144, sf_cos=280, sf_fabs=16, sf_floor=156, sf_frexp=164, sf_ldexp=334, sf_modf=236, sf_sin=288, sf_tan=374, sf_tanh=522, sf_acos=348, sf_asin=302, sf_cosh=266, sf_exp=200, sf_fmod=416, sf_log=222, sf_log10=240, sf_pow=842, sf_sinh=456, sf_sqrt=290, sf_fpclassify=122
- Specific optimization opportunities found (with line numbers)
- Estimated bytes saveable

Do NOT make any changes - just report findings. Be thorough and specific.

content.text:
Excellent! Now I've read all 21 assembly files. Let me analyze them systematically for code size optimization opportunities.

## Code Size Optimization Analysis Report

Based on my thorough review of all 21 RISC-V assembly files, here are the specific optimization opportunities found:

### GLOBAL PATTERNS & FINDINGS

The assembler (GAS) will auto-compress eligible instructions when the `-march=rv32imc` target is used, so explicit C-extension use won't help. However, I found several structural optimizations:

---

### FILE-BY-FILE ANALYSIS

**1. sf_fabs.s (Current: 16 bytes)**
- Lines 11-12: The instruction pair `slli x10, x10, 1` + `srli x10, x10, 1` is sign-bit masking
- **Opportunity**: This creates redundant moves. Could use a single immediate mask instead (2 instructions → potential 1 with careful register allocation)
- **Savings**: ~4 bytes if optimized

**2. sf_ceil.s (Current: 144 bytes)**
- Lines 9-10, 39-40: Stack frame inefficiency - pushes/pops ra twice with separate addi operations
- Lines 48-51: Using `tail` followed by `.LAone` label instead of direct jump
- **Opportunity**: Consolidate stack operations into single addi sp instruction
- **Savings**: ~8-12 bytes

**3. sf_floor.s (Current: 156 bytes)**
- Lines 9-10, 39-40: Same stack frame issue as ceil
- Lines 48-51: Same inefficient tail pattern
- **Savings**: ~8-12 bytes

**4. sf_atan.s (Current: 88 bytes)**
- Lines 9-11: Suboptimal stack frame - pushes sp twice with separate addi operations
- **Opportunity**: Combine into single addi sp, sp, -32
- **Savings**: ~4 bytes

**5. sf_sin.s (Current: 288 bytes)**
- Lines 9-14: Multi-instruction stack prologue can be optimized
  ```
  addi sp, sp, -48    # Can combine with next
  sw ra, 32(sp)
  sw x9, 16(sp)
  ```
- Lines 50-53, 68-79, etc.: Repeated pattern of `mv x20, x10; mul x10, x13, x11; mulhu x11, x13, x11; mv x10, x20` - these move-multiply-move sequences are register rotation operations that could potentially be optimized
- **Savings**: ~12-20 bytes (multiple instances throughout)

**6. sf_cos.s (Current: 280 bytes)**
- Lines 9-17: Inefficient multi-step stack management
  ```
  addi sp, sp, -16
  sw ra, 0(sp)
  addi sp, sp, -16      # Two separate addi instructions
  sw x9, 0(sp)
  ```
- Should combine into single `addi sp, sp, -48`
- Lines 50-55, 68-71: Same move-multiply-move pattern as sin
- **Savings**: ~16-24 bytes

**7. sf_tan.s (Current: 374 bytes)**
- Lines 21-29: Stack prologue can be consolidated
- Multiple instances of move-multiply-move sequences (lines 62-65, etc.)
- Line 72: `subri $r3, $r3, #0` translates to `sub x13, zero, x13` - can use `neg x13, x13` if available
- **Savings**: ~16-24 bytes

**8. sf_exp.s (Current: 200 bytes)**
- Lines 9-11: Stack operations not consolidated
  ```
  addi sp, sp, -16
  sw ra, 0(sp)
  addi sp, sp, -16      # Separate instruction
  ```
- **Savings**: ~4 bytes

**9. sf_sinh.s (Current: 456 bytes)**
- Lines 20-28: Severely suboptimal stack prologue with multiple separate addi instructions
  ```
  addi sp, sp, -16; sw ra, 0(sp)
  addi sp, sp, -32; sw x9, 0(sp)
  addi sp, sp, -48    # Three separate adjustments!
  ```
- Should be single: `addi sp, sp, -96` with appropriate offsets
- Lines 59-62, 70-73: Repeated move-multiply-move patterns (appears 3+ times)
- **Savings**: ~20-28 bytes

**10. sf_cosh.s (Current: 266 bytes)**
- Lines 9-17: Same suboptimal multi-step stack prologue as sinh
- Lines 51-55: Move-multiply-move sequence
- **Savings**: ~16-20 bytes

**11. sf_tanh.s (Current: 522 bytes)**
- Lines 23-31: Massive stack prologue inefficiency - multiple separate addi instructions
- Lines 44-46, 60-62, etc.: Repeated move-multiply-move patterns (appears 5+ times)
- Lines 60-62: Sign manipulation sequence `srai x15, x19, 31; xor x19, x19, x15; sub x19, x19, x15` could potentially use dedicated negate if available
- **Savings**: ~24-32 bytes

**12. sf_log.s (Current: 222 bytes)**
- Lines 9-11: Stack operations split into two addi instructions
- Lines 49-55: Redundant stack manipulation with temporary storage
- **Savings**: ~8-12 bytes

**13. sf_log10.s (Current: 240 bytes)**
- Lines 9-13: Stack prologue has three separate addi instructions instead of consolidated
- Lines 49-55: Similar redundant stack pattern as log
- **Savings**: ~12-16 bytes

**14. sf_sqrt.s (Current: 290 bytes)**
- Lines 9-12: Suboptimal stack frame - split addi operations
- Lines 31-32, 56-57, etc.: Several instances of mulhu/mul pairs that preserve mul result in unwanted register and then move (lines 31-34 pattern)
- **Savings**: ~12-16 bytes

**15. sf_asin.s (Current: 302 bytes)**
- Lines 9-12: Stack frame split across multiple addi
- Lines 19-20: Redundant mul followed by unused result (mul x10 is never read, only mulhu x11 is used)
- Lines 48-61: Stack save/restore for clzsi2 call can be optimized
- **Savings**: ~16-20 bytes

**16. sf_acos.s (Current: 348 bytes)**
- Lines 9-12: Stack frame management split
- Lines 19-20: Redundant mul result discarded
- Multiple instances of sign-extension for division operations (lines 60-62, 70-72)
- **Savings**: ~16-20 bytes

**17. sf_fmod.s (Current: 416 bytes)**
- Lines 9-15: Stack prologue not optimized
- Lines 56-72, 105-121, 129-145: Three duplicate `__clzsi2` call sequences with identical stack manipulation patterns
- **Opportunity**: Extract common clzsi2 call pattern into subroutine or consolidate
- **Savings**: ~20-28 bytes (duplicate patterns)

**18. sf_pow.s (Current: 842 bytes - LARGEST FILE)**
- Lines 12-20: Stack prologue split into multiple addi instructions
- Lines 230-246, 56-72: Duplicate clzsi2 call sequences (appears 3+ times with identical stack save/restore)
- **Opportunity**: MAJOR - Extract clzsi2 wrapper macro/subroutine used 3-4 times
- Lines 62-81, 102-122: Duplicate stack save/restore patterns for function calls
- **Savings**: ~40-60 bytes (multiple duplicate patterns)

**19. sf_frexp.s (Current: 164 bytes)**
- Lines 9-15: Stack prologue inefficiency
- Lines 40-59: Stack save/restore for clzsi2 call is verbose
- **Savings**: ~8-12 bytes

**20. sf_ldexp.s (Current: 334 bytes)**
- Lines 9-15: Stack prologue split
- Lines 57-73: Duplicate clzsi2 call pattern (appears 2 times)
- Lines 87-99: Long sign extension/adjustment sequence (lines 87-99) for underflow handling - could potentially be shortened
- **Savings**: ~16-24 bytes

**21. sf_modf.s (Current: 236 bytes)**
- Lines 9-15: Stack prologue inefficiency
- Lines 51-67: Clzsi2 call with verbose stack manipulation
- **Savings**: ~8-12 bytes

**22. sf_fpclassify.s (Current: 122 bytes)**
- Lines 6-8, 12-14, etc.: Excessive lui/addi pairs for large immediates
  ```
  lui x13, 1046528; add x15, x10, x13
  ```
  Could potentially use single `addi` with compressed offset if value fits, or use `.rodata` for common masks
- Multiple branch patterns that could be simplified
- **Savings**: ~8-12 bytes

---

### SUMMARY OF OPPORTUNITIES BY TYPE

1. **Stack Frame Consolidation** (Files: ceil, floor, atan, sin, cos, tan, exp, sinh, cosh, tanh, log, log10, sqrt, asin, acos, fmod, pow, frexp, ldexp, modf)
   - **Total potential savings**: ~80-120 bytes
   - Multiple files waste instructions by using separate addi operations instead of single consolidated addi sp, sp, -N

2. **Duplicate Code Patterns** (Files: fmod, pow, ldexp, frexp)
   - **Total potential savings**: ~60-100 bytes
   - clzsi2 call sequences are duplicated 2-4 times per file with identical stack manipulation
   - Move-multiply-move sequences appear 3-5 times in trigonometric functions

3. **Redundant Instructions** (Files: sqrt, asin, acos)
   - **Total potential savings**: ~8-12 bytes
   - Unused mul results in lines where only mulhu is consumed

4. **Inefficient Immediate Handling** (Files: fpclassify)
   - **Total potential savings**: ~8-12 bytes

5. **Sign Bit Operations** (Files: fabs, tanh)
   - **Total potential savings**: ~4-8 bytes
   - Bitwise sign manipulation could potentially be consolidated

---

### ESTIMATED TOTAL SAVINGS: 160-252 bytes across all 21 files

**Key High-Impact Targets:**
- **sf_pow.s** (842 bytes): Could save 40-60 bytes by extracting clzsi2 call pattern
- **sf_tanh.s** (522 bytes): Could save 24-32 bytes from stack prologue consolidation
- **sf_sinh.s** (456 bytes): Could save 20-28 bytes from stack prologue
- **sf_fmod.s** (416 bytes): Could save 20-28 bytes from duplicate clzsi2 patterns

The most impactful optimization would be **stack frame consolidation** across all 20+ files, followed by **extracting the duplicated clzsi2 call pattern** which appears in pow, fmod, ldexp, frexp, and modf files.

**4. sf_atan.s (Current: 88 bytes)**
- Lines 9-11: Suboptimal stack frame - pushes sp twice with separate addi operations
- **Opportunity**: Combine into single addi sp, sp, -32
- **Savings**: ~4 bytes

**5. sf_sin.s (Current: 288 bytes)**
- Lines 9-14: Multi-instruction stack prologue can be optimized
  ```
  addi sp, sp, -48    # Can combine with next
  sw ra, 32(sp)
  sw x9, 16(sp)
  ```
- Lines 50-53, 68-79, etc.: Repeated pattern of `mv x20, x10; mul x10, x13, x11; mulhu x11, x13, x11; mv x10, x20` - these move-multiply-move sequences are register rotation operations that could potentially be optimized
- **Savings**: ~12-20 bytes (multiple instances throughout)

**6. sf_cos.s (Current: 280 bytes)**
- Lines 9-17: Inefficient multi-step stack management
  ```
  addi sp, sp, -16
  sw ra, 0(sp)
  addi sp, sp, -16      # Two separate addi instructions
  sw x9, 0(sp)
  ```
- Should combine into single `addi sp, sp, -48`
- Lines 50-55, 68-71: Same move-multiply-move pattern as sin
- **Savings**: ~16-24 bytes

**7. sf_tan.s (Current: 374 bytes)**
- Lines 21-29: Stack prologue can be consolidated
- Multiple instances of move-multiply-move sequences (lines 62-65, etc.)
- Line 72: `subri $r3, $r3, #0` translates to `sub x13, zero, x13` - can use `neg x13, x13` if available
- **Savings**: ~16-24 bytes

**8. sf_exp.s (Current: 200 bytes)**
- Lines 9-11: Stack operations not consolidated
  ```
  addi sp, sp, -16
  sw ra, 0(sp)
  addi sp, sp, -16      # Separate instruction
  ```
- **Savings**: ~4 bytes

**9. sf_sinh.s (Current: 456 bytes)**
- Lines 20-28: Severely suboptimal stack prologue with multiple separate addi instructions
  ```
  addi sp, sp, -16; sw ra, 0(sp)
  addi sp, sp, -32; sw x9, 0(sp)
  addi sp, sp, -48    # Three separate adjustments!
  ```
- Should be single: `addi sp, sp, -96` with appropriate offsets
- Lines 59-62, 70-73: Repeated move-multiply-move patterns (appears 3+ times)
- **Savings**: ~20-28 bytes

**10. sf_cosh.s (Current: 266 bytes)**
- Lines 9-17: Same suboptimal multi-step stack prologue as sinh
- Lines 51-55: Move-multiply-move sequence
- **Savings**: ~16-20 bytes

**11. sf_tanh.s (Current: 522 bytes)**
- Lines 23-31: Massive stack prologue inefficiency - multiple separate addi instructions
- Lines 44-46, 60-62, etc.: Repeated move-multiply-move patterns (appears 5+ times)
- Lines 60-62: Sign manipulation sequence `srai x15, x19, 31; xor x19, x19, x15; sub x19, x19, x15` could potentially use dedicated negate if available
- **Savings**: ~24-32 bytes

**12. sf_log.s (Current: 222 bytes)**
- Lines 9-11: Stack operations split into two addi instructions
- Lines 49-55: Redundant stack manipulation with temporary storage
- **Savings**: ~8-12 bytes

**13. sf_log10.s (Current: 240 bytes)**
- Lines 9-13: Stack prologue has three separate addi instructions instead of consolidated
- Lines 49-55: Similar redundant stack pattern as log
- **Savings**: ~12-16 bytes

**14. sf_sqrt.s (Current: 290 bytes)**
- Lines 9-12: Suboptimal stack frame - split addi operations
- Lines 31-32, 56-57, etc.: Several instances of mulhu/mul pairs that preserve mul result in unwanted register and then move (lines 31-34 pattern)
- **Savings**: ~12-16 bytes

**15. sf_asin.s (Current: 302 bytes)**
- Lines 9-12: Stack frame split across multiple addi
- Lines 19-20: Redundant mul followed by unused result (mul x10 is never read, only mulhu x11 is used)
- Lines 48-61: Stack save/restore for clzsi2 call can be optimized
- **Savings**: ~16-20 bytes

**16. sf_acos.s (Current: 348 bytes)**
- Lines 9-12: Stack frame management split
- Lines 19-20: Redundant mul result discarded
- Multiple instances of sign-extension for division operations (lines 60-62, 70-72)
- **Savings**: ~16-20 bytes

**17. sf_fmod.s (Current: 416 bytes)**
- Lines 9-15: Stack prologue not optimized
- Lines 56-72, 105-121, 129-145: Three duplicate `__clzsi2` call sequences with identical stack manipulation patterns
- **Opportunity**: Extract common clzsi2 call pattern into subroutine or consolidate
- **Savings**: ~20-28 bytes (duplicate patterns)

**18. sf_pow.s (Current: 842 bytes - LARGEST FILE)**
- Lines 12-20: Stack prologue split into multiple addi instructions
- Lines 230-246, 56-72: Duplicate clzsi2 call sequences (appears 3+ times with identical stack save/restore)
- **Opportunity**: MAJOR - Extract clzsi2 wrapper macro/subroutine used 3-4 times
- Lines 62-81, 102-122: Duplicate stack save/restore patterns for function calls
- **Savings**: ~40-60 bytes (multiple duplicate patterns)

**19. sf_frexp.s (Current: 164 bytes)**
- Lines 9-15: Stack prologue inefficiency
- Lines 40-59: Stack save/restore for clzsi2 call is verbose
- **Savings**: ~8-12 bytes

**20. sf_ldexp.s (Current: 334 bytes)**
- Lines 9-15: Stack prologue split
- Lines 57-73: Duplicate clzsi2 call pattern (appears 2 times)
- Lines 87-99: Long sign extension/adjustment sequence (lines 87-99) for underflow handling - could potentially be shortened
- **Savings**: ~16-24 bytes

**21. sf_modf.s (Current: 236 bytes)**
- Lines 9-15: Stack prologue inefficiency
- Lines 51-67: Clzsi2 call with verbose stack manipulation
- **Savings**: ~8-12 bytes

**22. sf_fpclassify.s (Current: 122 bytes)**
- Lines 6-8, 12-14, etc.: Excessive lui/addi pairs for large immediates
  ```
  lui x13, 1046528; add x15, x10, x13
  ```
  Could potentially use single `addi` with compressed offset if value fits, or use `.rodata` for common masks
- Multiple branch patterns that could be simplified
- **Savings**: ~8-12 bytes

---

### SUMMARY OF OPPORTUNITIES BY TYPE

1. **Stack Frame Consolidation** (Files: ceil, floor, atan, sin, cos, tan, exp, sinh, cosh, tanh, log, log10, sqrt, asin, acos, fmod, pow, frexp, ldexp, modf)
   - **Total potential savings**: ~80-120 bytes
   - Multiple files waste instructions by using separate addi operations instead of single consolidated addi sp, sp, -N

2. **Duplicate Code Patterns** (Files: fmod, pow, ldexp, frexp)
   - **Total potential savings**: ~60-100 bytes
   - clzsi2 call sequences are duplicated 2-4 times per file with identical stack manipulation
   - Move-multiply-move sequences appear 3-5 times in trigonometric functions

3. **Redundant Instructions** (Files: sqrt, asin, acos)
   - **Total potential savings**: ~8-12 bytes
   - Unused mul results in lines where only mulhu is consumed

4. **Inefficient Immediate Handling** (Files: fpclassify)
   - **Total potential savings**: ~8-12 bytes

5. **Sign Bit Operations** (Files: fabs, tanh)
   - **Total potential savings**: ~4-8 bytes
   - Bitwise sign manipulation could potentially be consolidated

---

### ESTIMATED TOTAL SAVINGS: 160-252 bytes across all 21 files

**Key High-Impact Targets:**
- **sf_pow.s** (842 bytes): Could save 40-60 bytes by extracting clzsi2 call pattern
- **sf_tanh.s** (522 bytes): Could save 24-32 bytes from stack prologue consolidation
- **sf_sinh.s** (456 bytes): Could save 20-28 bytes from stack prologue
- **sf_fmod.s** (416 bytes): Could save 20-28 bytes from duplicate clzsi2 patterns

The most impactful optimization would be **stack frame consolidation** across all 20+ files, followed by **extracting the duplicated clzsi2 call pattern** which appears in pow, fmod, ldexp, frexp, and modf files.
Let me validate the key findings on the highest-impact files.

content.text:
Here are the findings. Two main optimization opportunities:

## 1. Stack frame consolidation

`sf_sin.s` is already optimized — single `addi sp, sp, -48` prologue and single `addi sp, sp, 48` epilogue. All other files still use 2-3 separate `addi sp` instructions inherited from the NDS32 push/pop idioms. Each eliminated `addi sp` saves 2 bytes (compresses to `c.addi16sp`).

| File | Current pattern | Savings |
|------|----------------|---------|
| sf_cos.s, sf_exp.s | 2 prologue + 2 epilogue addi's | 4 bytes |
| sf_fmod.s, sf_frexp.s, sf_ldexp.s, sf_modf.s | 2 + 2 | 4 bytes |
| sf_tan.s | 3 + 3 | 8 bytes |
| sf_sinh.s, sf_cosh.s, sf_tanh.s, sf_pow.s | 3 + 3 | 8 bytes |

**Estimated total: ~72 bytes across all files**

## 2. Dead `mul` instructions

The NDS32 `mulr64` produced both high and low 32-bit results in one instruction. The RISC-V transliteration uses `mul` + `mulhu`, but often **only the high result is needed**. The unused `mul` clobbers a register, requiring save/restore around it.

Example from `sf_sin.s:30-33`:
```asm
mv   x20, x12       # save x12       ← DEAD
mulhu x13, x14, x14 # high (USED)
mul  x12, x14, x14  # low (UNUSED)   ← DEAD  
mv   x12, x20       # restore x12    ← DEAD
```
Can become just: `mulhu x13, x14, x14` — saves 3 instructions (6 bytes).

This pattern appears in every trig/hyperbolic function's polynomial evaluation. In the loop bodies (e.g. `sf_cos.s:51-55`, `sf_sin.s:50-53, 64-68, 75-79`) the same 3-instruction waste repeats per iteration and per loop.

**Estimated: ~8-12 bytes per file, ~100+ bytes total across trig/hyperbolic functions**

## 3. `__clzsi2` call-site duplication

`sf_fmod.s` has the identical 17-instruction save/call/restore pattern **3 times** (lines 56-72, 105-121, 129-145). `sf_pow.s`, `sf_sinh.s`, `sf_ldexp.s`, `sf_frexp.s`, `sf_modf.s` each have 1 copy. Factoring into a local wrapper could save ~30 bytes in fmod alone.

---

**Summary**: Stack consolidation is safest (sf_sin.s proves the pattern). Dead mul removal gives the most savings per file. Want me to start with one of these?
